* identity of `.pc` files used;
* resolved compiler and linker flags used from `pkg-config`;
* header file digests after resolving include paths;
* transitive header or module-map digests required to derive the raw surface;
* preprocessor definitions;
* target triple;
* data layout;
* calling convention;
* selected ABI language mode;
* binding generator identity and version;
* trusted binding summary identity;
* shim source digests;
* prebuilt library identities;
* selected native adapter mode;
* selected link and load specifications.

A resolved host binding provider records a host-source identity.

For JVM bindings, host-source identity includes, when applicable:

* resolved Maven/JAR artifact identities;
* classfile digests;
* module descriptor digests;
* selected classpath or module-path entries;
* selected JVM release or bytecode target;
* binding generator identity;
* trusted binding summary identity;
* generated binding interface identity.

For .NET bindings, host-source identity includes, when applicable:

* resolved NuGet package identities;
* selected CLR assembly identities;
* metadata digests;
* selected target framework;
* selected runtime identifier;
* binding generator identity;
* trusted binding summary identity;
* generated binding interface identity.

For Python bindings, host-source identity includes, when applicable:

* resolved Python dependency graph identity;
* selected Python implementation family;
* selected Python version or version range;
* selected ABI or build-mode assumptions;
* selected GIL/free-threaded assumptions;
* selected virtual-environment or environment layout identity, when materialized;
* selected package distribution identities;
* selected distribution file digests;
* selected extras and evaluated environment markers;
* selected type-stub identities;
* selected import-introspection transcript identity;
* selected generated wrapper metadata identity;
* selected top-level Python module names;
* binding generator identity;
* trusted binding summary identity;
* adapter mode identity;
* thread, event-loop, interpreter, and GIL-affinity policy;
* exception and error-mapping policy;
* blocking-call classification policy;
* callback ingress policy; and
* generated binding interface identity.

For native bindings, generated binding cache entries have the following portable shape:

```text
GeneratedBindingLockEntry:
    host binding provider identity
    host root
    binding source identity
    header/module-map/symbol-list digests
    resolved pkg-config identity, when any
    trusted binding summary identity
    generator identity and version
    target ABI identity
    adapter mode identity
    selected link specification
    selected load specification
    generated Kappa raw interface identity
    generated source or interface artifact identity
    cache key
```

A later build MAY reuse a generated binding only if every recorded input in the corresponding generated binding lock
entry is identical.

For Python bindings, generated binding cache entries have the following portable shape:

```text
GeneratedPythonBindingLockEntry:
    host binding provider identity
    host root
    binding source identity
    resolved Python dependency graph identity
    Python implementation family
    Python version or version range
    Python ABI or build-mode assumptions
    GIL/free-threaded assumptions
    package distribution identities
    distribution file digests
    selected extras and environment markers
    type-stub identities
    import-introspection transcript identity, when any
    trusted binding summary identity
    generator identity and version
    adapter mode identity
    exception/error mapping policy
    blocking classification policy
    affinity policy
    generated Kappa raw interface identity
    generated source or interface artifact identity
    cache key
```

A later build MAY reuse a generated Python binding only if every recorded input in the corresponding generated binding
lock entry is identical.

A generated raw binding interface identity MUST be explicit in the lockfile or equivalent artifact. It MUST NOT be
reconstructed solely from generator version and header digests when additional trusted summaries, ABI details, adapter
mode, or implementation options can affect the generated interface.

A host binding provider MUST NOT silently infer ownership, nullability, thread-affinity, callback behavior, error
mapping, blocking behavior, lifetime, or release behavior from headers alone.

Such facts require a trusted binding summary, shim, refined overlay module, bridge contract, or conservative raw surface.

If the required precision is unavailable, the generated raw binding must use conservative types such as `Option`,
`std.ffi.RawPtr`, `std.ffi.OpaqueHandle`, checked boundary results, dynamic values, opaque Python object wrappers, or
another explicitly imprecise surface permitted by this specification.

<!-- build_system.deployment_native_runtime -->
### 36.29 Deployment, native loading, and runtime prerequisites

A deployment mode describes whether produced artifacts are self-contained, thin, bundled, system-dependent, or
implementation-defined.

Portable deployment modes include:

```text
self-contained
thin
bundled
system-prerequisite
provided-by-host
implementation-defined
```

A native load specification contributes to deployment mode but does not replace it.

A bundled native dependency records:

* native library identity;
* producer dependency or target;
* selected ABI identity;
* selected library file identity;
* deployment-relative path;
* runtime loader policy;
* generated loader metadata;
* platform-specific install name, rpath, loader path, or equivalent metadata when applicable.

A system-provided native dependency records:

* required library or SDK name;
* version constraint or compatibility constraint when known;
* ABI identity or compatibility fingerprint when known;
* header or metadata identity used for compilation;
* runtime prerequisite description;
* non-self-contained deployment status.

`system-loader` means runtime loading uses the platform's ordinary loader search behavior.

`bundled-loader` means the deployment layout records the exact bundled native library identity and deployment path.

`provided-by-host` means the selected runtime, platform, SDK, or host process is expected to supply the required symbol
surface or library handle.

A build that uses `system-loader` or `provided-by-host` MUST NOT claim to be self-contained unless every such dependency
is also bundled or otherwise made part of the deployment layout.

A build may be reproducible for compilation while intentionally non-self-contained for deployment. In that case the
target reproducibility status is `ReproducibleCompilationButSystemRuntimePrerequisite` or `NonSelfContainedDeployment`,
not `FullyReproducible`.

Runtime prerequisite diagnostics MUST distinguish:

* missing compile-time headers or metadata;
* missing link-time library;
* missing runtime library;
* incompatible runtime ABI;
* missing platform SDK;
* missing host runtime;
* missing device driver;
* unsupported loader policy;
* non-self-contained deployment.

<!-- build_system.bridges -->
### 36.30 Bridge providers and bridge targets

A bridge target realizes one bridge between provider and consumer artifacts, targets, or artifact identities.

A bridge target has at least:

* bridge target name;
* provider target reference or provider artifact dependency;
* consumer target reference, consumer backend profile, or consumer artifact dependency;
* exported module set or signature surface;
* imported module names or aliases;
* backend pair;
* bridge realization mode;
* bridge contract policy;
* callback policy;
* generated companion artifact policy;
* deployment prerequisites;
* runtime prerequisite policy.

Portable bridge realization names include, where implemented:

```text
kappa.jni
    Kappa-to-Kappa bridge between JVM and native artifacts using JNI or the JVM Invocation API.

kappa.clr-native
    Kappa-to-Kappa bridge between .NET/CLR and native artifacts using a CLR native interop realization such as
    P/Invoke, reverse P/Invoke, generated native exports, or an implementation-defined equivalent recorded in the
    bridge contract.

kappa.wasm-component-host
    Kappa-to-Kappa bridge between a WASM component artifact and a native, JVM, .NET, or other host artifact using the
    WASM component model interface recorded in the bridge contract.

kappa.c-abi-adapter
    Kappa-to-Kappa or Kappa-to-foreign bridge through an explicitly declared C ABI adapter surface. This realization is
    limited by the selected C ABI export/import surface and does not preserve full Kappa precision unless the adapter
    contract explicitly proves that precision.
```

Standardizing a bridge realization name does not require every implementation to support that realization.

An implementation that advertises support for a standardized bridge realization MUST support the minimum metadata,
callback-policy, deployment-prerequisite, generated-companion, and rejection-diagnostic requirements specified for that
realization.

A bridge target does not have one backend profile.

A bridge target records the provider backend profile and consumer backend profile as a backend pair.

A bridge target that supplies static modules to a consumer target creates bridge-supplied Kappa modules under §8.3.6.

The provider artifact MUST supply Kappa module interface artifacts for every bridge-supplied module.

The consumer target typechecks against those module interfaces exactly as it would against ordinary separately compiled
Kappa modules.

The bridge realization MUST supply or derive a bridge contract sufficient to preserve the exported Kappa surface.

A bridge target MUST NOT silently degrade a precise Kappa surface to `Dyn`, raw pointers, opaque handles, erased typed
errors, serialized blobs, or lossy foreign values.

If the selected bridge realization cannot preserve or enforce the requested surface, build-plan resolution MUST reject
the bridge unless the user explicitly selects a less precise adapter module.

A bridge target may emit companion artifacts on the provider side, consumer side, or deployment side.

Companion bridge artifacts include, when applicable:

* JNI entry points;
* native bridge stubs;
* JVM companion classes;
* .NET companion assemblies;
* registration tables;
* callback trampolines;
* runtime representation tables;
* bridge contract tables;
* loader metadata;
* deployment manifests.

Generated bridge companion artifacts are companion artifacts. They do not make the bridge target an artifact target.

A bridge target whose contract permits callbacks from consumer into provider, or provider into consumer, MUST record:

* callback surface identity;
* callback lifetime policy;
* callback ownership policy;
* callback threading policy;
* callback exception/error/failure mapping;
* callback registration and release policy;
* whether callbacks may outlive the dynamic invocation that supplied them;
* rejection rules for unsupported callback shapes.

A bridge target used to connect bidirectional runtime interaction MUST NOT create a static module import cycle.

Bidirectional runtime interaction across bridge-connected targets MUST use one of:

* a shared acyclic interface package imported by both targets;
* callback values passed through one direction of the bridge;
* runtime bridge handles and `std.bridge` package values;
* an implementation-documented bridge-bundle mechanism explicitly standardized by that implementation.

Portable v1 does not standardize mutually recursive static bridge bundles.

In package mode, the lockfile or equivalent artifact MUST record at least:

* bridge target identity;
* provider target or artifact identity;
* consumer target, backend, or artifact identity;
* exported module interface identities;
* bridge contract identity;
* bridge generator identity;
* backend pair;
* bridge realization mode;
* callback policy identity;
* generated companion artifact identities when materialized;
* deployment prerequisites;
* native, managed, WASM, or bridge runtime identities required by the bridge when available.

<!-- build_system.bridges.scheduling_phases -->
#### 36.30.1 Bridge scheduling phases

A bridge target is resolved into phase outputs so that bridge-supplied modules do not create artificial target cycles.

Portable bridge phases are:

```text
BridgeContractPlan
BridgeConsumerCompanions
BridgeProviderCompanions
BridgeDeploymentMetadata
```

`BridgeContractPlan` records the selected bridge realization and bridge contract before provider or consumer final
packaging.

It depends on:

* provider Kappa interface surface or exported signature surface;
* provider backend profile;
* consumer backend profile or consumer artifact identity;
* selected bridge realization;
* selected callback policy;
* selected runtime representation policy;
* selected error/exception mapping policy;
* selected deployment policy; and
* relevant lockfile or transcript entries.

`BridgeConsumerCompanions` are generated modules, stubs, wrappers, registration declarations, metadata, or interface
artifacts required before compiling or packaging the consumer side.

They depend on the `BridgeContractPlan` and on the consumer-side backend/profile context.

A consumer artifact target that statically imports a bridge-supplied Kappa module depends on
`BridgeConsumerCompanions` and on the provider module interface identities recorded by the `BridgeContractPlan`. It
does not depend on provider final deployment metadata merely to typecheck the imported Kappa module.

`BridgeProviderCompanions` are generated entry points, wrappers, callback trampolines, registration tables, export
metadata, native stubs, managed stubs, or other artifacts required before final provider-side linking or packaging.

They depend on the `BridgeContractPlan` and on the provider-side backend/profile context.

A provider artifact target MAY have a split build shape:

```text
provider compile/interface phase
provider bridge companion phase
provider final link/package phase
```

When such a split is used, the `BridgeContractPlan` may depend on the provider compile/interface phase, and the
provider final link/package phase may depend on `BridgeProviderCompanions`, without forming a target dependency cycle.

`BridgeDeploymentMetadata` records loader metadata, bundled runtime files, runtime prerequisites, deployment-relative
paths, registration metadata, bridge runtime identities, and final provider/consumer artifact identities.

It depends on:

* provider final artifact identity when required by deployment;
* consumer final artifact identity when required by deployment;
* `BridgeContractPlan`;
* materialized provider companions;
* materialized consumer companions; and
* deployment layout resolution.

A bridge target creates a target dependency cycle only when a phase dependency cycle remains after expanding the bridge
target into the phase outputs above.

Portable v1 does not standardize mutually recursive static bridge bundles. Any implementation-defined bridge-bundle
mechanism MUST define an equivalent phase decomposition and MUST record it in the resolved plan.

<!-- build_system.test_targets -->
### 36.31 Test targets

A test target describes compiler checks, compile checks, runtime tests, integration tests, diagnostic tests, golden
tests, or compile-fail suites.

Portable test kinds are:

```text
unit
integration
compile
compile-fail
diagnostic
golden
bridge
host-capability
```

A test target has at least:

* target name;
* test kind;
* selected source roots or test roots;
* selected suite specs;
* dependencies;
* target artifact dependencies under test;
* selected features;
* selected host bindings, if any;
* selected bridge providers, if any;
* expected unsupported/rejected/skipped policy;
* one or more test executions.

A test execution target has at least:

* execution name;
* backend profile;
* artifact family used for test execution, when applicable;
* fragment tags;
* build profile;
* unsafe/debug policy;
* runtime parameters;
* runtime prerequisites;
* expected result policy.

A test execution target has exactly one backend profile.

A test target with multiple backend executions resolves to multiple test execution targets.

A diagnostic test target has at least:

* selected source roots;
* package mode or script mode;
* backend profile context;
* selected fragment tags;
* selected host binding availability;
* selected unsafe/debug policy;
* expected diagnostic codes;
* expected primary spans;
* expected labels;
* expected notes or helps, when asserted;
* expected fix-its, when asserted.

A golden test target has at least:

* selected source roots;
* selected compiler stage or emitted artifact kind;
* canonicalization policy;
* golden file identity;
* expected update policy.

A compile-fail test target is a diagnostic test target whose expected result is rejection.

Test results MUST be grouped by resolved test target and resolved test execution target.

A test result record MUST include the resolved-plan projection used for that execution.

A test target does not by itself produce a runtime artifact unless one of its test execution targets explicitly compiles
or depends on an artifact target.

A test target MUST NOT hide unsupported backends as passing tests.

Unsupported, skipped, rejected, failed, and passed are distinct outcomes.

<!-- build_system.benchmark_targets -->
### 36.32 Benchmark targets

A benchmark target describes reproducible benchmark compilation and execution.

A benchmark target has at least:

* target name;
* benchmark suite spec;
* subject target or benchmark module selector;
* dependencies;
* selected features;
* one or more benchmark executions;
* dataset identities;
* runtime parameters;
* result attribution policy.

A benchmark execution has at least:

* execution name;
* backend profile;
* primary artifact family used for benchmark execution;
* build profile;
* optimization profile;
* optional PGO policy;
* optional LTO policy;
* optional sanitizer policy;
* optional profiling policy;
* runtime parameters;
* runtime prerequisites;
* environment prerequisites.

A benchmark execution has exactly one backend profile.

A benchmark dataset records:

* dataset name;
* source;
* immutable content identity or transcript identity;
* decompression or preparation step identity, when any;
* logical input name exposed to the benchmark.

Benchmark runtime parameters record:

* arguments;
* environment supplied by the build tool;
* working directory policy;
* warmup policy;
* measurement policy;
* iteration policy;
* timeout policy;
* concurrency policy.

A benchmark result MUST be attributable to:

* resolved benchmark target;
* resolved benchmark execution;
* resolved plan projection;
* backend profile;
* build profile;
* optimization profile;
* toolchain identity;
* runtime identity;
* dataset identity;
* runtime parameters;
* host or system prerequisites that the benchmark result declares relevant.

Benchmark results are not part of ordinary compilation semantics, but benchmark configuration is part of the resolved
plan when the benchmark target is selected.

<!-- build_system.publish_targets -->
### 36.33 Publish targets

A publish target describes packaging and publication of already built or already resolved artifacts.

A publish target has at least:

* target name;
* package or workspace package selection;
* artifact selection;
* package metadata selection;
* dependency metadata policy;
* path dependency policy;
* lockfile inclusion policy;
* provenance inclusion policy;
* reproducibility policy;
* signing or attestation policy, when any;
* repository or destination selection; and
* dry-run or publication execution policy.

A publish target has no backend profile.

A publish target MUST NOT compile modules.

A publish target MUST NOT select a different backend profile, artifact family, export surface, dependency graph,
provider alternative, host binding, bridge contract, generated output, or deployment layout from the one recorded in
the `ResolvedBuildPlanFinal` for the artifacts being published.

A publish artifact record includes at least:

* producing target identity;
* artifact identity;
* primary artifact family;
* companion artifact identities selected for publication;
* export surface identities;
* module interface identities;
* backend profile;
* toolchain identity;
* dependency graph identity;
* host binding identities;
* bridge identities;
* generated output identities;
* deployment layout identity, when publishing a deployment; and
* reproducibility vector.

A publish target MUST reject an artifact whose reproducibility summary is `Unreproducible`.

A publish target MUST reject an artifact whose lockfile entries, generated-output cache entries, host binding
identities, bridge identities, or dependency identities are missing or stale.

A publish target MAY publish an artifact with recorded system runtime prerequisites only if the published metadata
explicitly records those prerequisites and marks the artifact or deployment as non-self-contained.

Path dependencies in publish mode are governed by the publish target's path dependency policy:

```text
reject-path-dependencies
record-path-content-identities
vendor-path-dependencies
implementation-defined-path-policy
```

Portable publish mode defaults to `reject-path-dependencies`.

If `record-path-content-identities` is selected, every path dependency reachable from the published artifact MUST be
represented by content identity sufficient to reproduce the dependency's source, manifest, selected generated outputs,
and exported interfaces.

If `vendor-path-dependencies` is selected, the published package MUST include the vendored dependency contents and
record their content identities.

A publish plan records every package metadata field emitted, the manifest value or generated value that supplied it,
and the provenance for that value.

<!-- build_system.module_providers -->
### 36.34 Module providers

A module provider is a selected source of effective Kappa modules for a target.

Provider kinds include:

* selected source fragments from the package itself;
* selected source fragments from package dependencies;
* URL modules resolved under §8.3.2;
* host binding modules under §8.3.5;
* bridge-supplied Kappa modules under §8.3.6;
* generated source modules or generated interface modules produced by selected host bindings or bridge realizations.

For a target, module discovery MUST resolve every effective module name to at most one selected provider.

A provider collision is a compile-time error unless the build configuration explicitly selects, aliases, or excludes
providers so that exactly one provider remains for the effective module name.

Provider collision diagnostics MUST identify:

* effective module name;
* every provider claiming that module name;
* provider kind;
* dependency, source root, host binding, bridge, generated artifact, or URL source that introduced each provider;
* target being built;
* build-config source origin or value provenance that selected or failed to select the provider.

An alias MUST NOT cause two different module interfaces to masquerade as one semantic module identity.

<!-- build_system.module_providers.identity_collision -->
#### 36.34.1 Provider identity and collision checking

A resolved module provider identity records at least:

* provider kind;
* effective module name;
* source package or artifact identity;
* source root or generated root identity when applicable;
* selected fragment file identities when applicable;
* module interface identity;
* host binding provider identity when applicable;
* bridge target and bridge phase identity when applicable;
* generated output identity when applicable;
* provider alias identity when applicable;
* selected feature set that enabled the provider;
* target for which the provider is selected; and
* provenance of the build value that selected or introduced the provider.

Collision checking is performed after feature resolution, matrix expansion, provider alternative resolution,
generated-output materialization required for module discovery, and provider alias resolution.

For each target, every effective module name MUST map to at most one selected provider identity.

Two providers with byte-identical source text are still distinct providers unless their resolved provider identities are
the same.

Two providers with the same module interface fingerprint are still distinct providers unless their resolved provider
identities are the same.

A provider alternative group may resolve a collision only by selecting, aliasing, or excluding providers before final
provider collision checking.

A provider exclusion removes a provider only for the target and module-provider scope in which the exclusion is active.
It does not remove the dependency, artifact, host binding, or bridge from the resolved plan unless no other selected
target uses it.

<!-- build_system.module_providers.aliases -->
### 36.35 Provider aliases

A provider alias creates an additional effective module name for one selected module provider.

A resolved provider alias records at least:

* original effective module name;
* alias effective module name;
* original provider identity;
* original module interface identity;
* alias declaration provenance;
* target in which the alias is active; and
* alias compatibility fingerprint.

A provider alias does not rewrite declaration semantic identities.

A provider alias does not create a new provider identity.

A provider alias does not permit two different provider identities to share one effective module identity.

A provider alias contributes to import resolution exactly as if the aliased module interface were reachable under the
alias effective module name, but every exported declaration retains its original semantic identity.

If a module interface identity contains self-module references, those references remain references to the original
provider identity unless the alias explicitly declares a self-aliasing policy. Portable v1 does not standardize
self-alias rewriting.

Two aliases collide when they introduce the same alias effective module name for different provider identities in the
same target.

An alias collision is a provider collision.

The module interface identity visible through an alias records both the original provider identity and the alias
effective module name. This prevents two aliases of distinct modules from masquerading as one semantic module.

<!-- build_system.query_integration -->
### 36.36 Query integration

The `ResolvedBuildPlan` is part of the effective build configuration used by the query model of §34.2.8.

A query result whose answer can depend on any of the following MUST include the corresponding `ResolvedBuildPlan`
projection in its query key:

* selected source files;
* selected source roots;
* selected fragment tags;
* selected dependencies;
* dependency module interface identities;
* selected module providers;
* host binding surfaces;
* bridge-supplied modules;
* bridge contracts;
* backend profile;
* backend intrinsic set;
* selected Python runtime identity;
* selected Python dependency graph identity;
* selected Python GIL/free-threaded deployment mode;
* native adapter mode;
* deployment mode;
* unsafe/debug policy;
* package mode vs script mode;
* lockfile identities;
* external discovery transcripts.

Changing the `BuildConfig`, build request, lockfile, resolved dependency identity, selected backend, host binding
identity, bridge identity, or source-root membership invalidates all affected queries from the earliest affected phase.

The implementation MUST NOT maintain a separate build graph whose target selection, module providers, host bindings,
bridge selections, or backend profiles can disagree with the `ResolvedBuildPlan`.

Additional query-key inputs:

A query result whose answer can depend on any of the following MUST also include the corresponding
`ResolvedBuildPlanFinal` projection in its query key:

* `std.build` schema identity;
* selected feature fixed point;
* matrix-generated target identity and axis values;
* interface surface fingerprint;
* provider alias identity;
* full reproducibility record under §36.6;
* lockfile update mode;
* lockfile entry identities consulted by the target;
* generated-output cache entries consulted by the target;
* generated Python source layout identity;
* generated Python runtime-library identity;
* generated Python wrapper identity;
* generated Python binding lock entries consulted by the target;
* materialization-step identities required by the target;
* macro dependency identities;
* macro transcript identities;
* codegen transcript identities;
* bridge phase identities;
* deployment layout identity;
* runtime prerequisite identities; and
* publish plan projection when the query concerns publish metadata.

A compiler query MUST NOT key only on the authored `BuildConfig` when resolution can select a different dependency
identity, provider identity, generated output, host binding, bridge realization, backend profile, feature set, or
deployment prerequisite.

If a query is valid for a `BuildPlanDraft` only, its key MUST identify that it is a planning query and MUST NOT be
reused as a compilation query key for `ResolvedBuildPlanFinal`.

<!-- build_system.explanation_queries -->
### 36.37 Build explanation queries

A conforming implementation MUST expose explanation queries for at least:

```text
whyTargetSelected(target)
whyFeatureEnabled(package, feature, target)
whyDependencySelected(package, target)
whyProviderSelected(moduleName, target)
whyActionRebuilt(action)
whyActionCached(action)
whyNotReproducible(target)
whyVariantSelected(edge)
whyBridgeSelected(bridgeTarget)
whyRuntimePrerequisite(requiredBy)
```

Each explanation query MUST return structured provenance, not prose only.

<!-- build_system.event_stream -->
### 36.38 Build event stream

A conforming implementation intended for build-tool, editor, CI, or package-registry integration SHOULD expose a
structured build event stream.

A `BuildEventStream` records at least:

* event stream schema version;
* invocation identity;
* build request identity;
* resolved plan identity when available;
* event sequence;
* parent-child event relationships;
* target identities;
* action identities;
* artifact identities;
* diagnostic identities;
* reproducibility records;
* provenance records;
* timestamps or deterministic ordering policy; and
* final build outcome.

Portable build event kinds include:

```text
BuildStarted
ConfigEvaluationStarted
ConfigEvaluationFinished
ResolutionStarted
ResolutionFinished
MaterializationStarted
MaterializationFinished
TargetScheduled
TargetStarted
TargetFinished
ActionStarted
ActionFinished
DiagnosticEmitted
ArtifactProduced
TestStarted
TestFinished
BenchmarkStarted
BenchmarkFinished
PublishStarted
PublishFinished
BuildFinished
```

Tools MUST NOT parse human-readable log output to recover information available in the build event stream.

<!-- build_system.diagnostics -->
### 36.39 Build diagnostics

Build diagnostics are ordinary compiler diagnostics.

A build diagnostic MUST include, when applicable:

* build manifest file;
* config profile;
* target name;
* package or dependency name;
* selected backend profile;
* selected deployment mode;
* source root or source file;
* host binding provider;
* bridge name;
* lockfile entry;
* primary source origin in `kappa.build.kp`;
* relevant config value provenance.

Required diagnostic classes include:

```text
E_BUILD_CONFIG_EXPECTED
E_BUILD_SCHEMA_INCOMPATIBLE
E_BUILD_TARGET_UNKNOWN
E_BUILD_TARGET_DUPLICATE
E_BUILD_TARGET_KIND_INVALID
E_BUILD_TARGET_BACKEND_RULE_VIOLATED
E_BUILD_TARGET_ARTIFACT_INCOMPATIBLE
E_BUILD_MATRIX_COMBINATION_INVALID
E_BUILD_MATRIX_TARGET_NAME_COLLISION
E_BUILD_FRAGMENT_TAG_UNKNOWN
E_BUILD_FRAGMENT_AXIS_CONFLICT
E_BUILD_FRAGMENT_COMPOSITION_AMBIGUOUS
E_BUILD_FRAGMENT_FIXITY_CONFLICT
E_BUILD_SOURCE_TREE_UNPINNED
E_BUILD_RESOURCE_COLLISION
E_BUILD_SOURCE_COLLISION
E_BUILD_PROVIDER_COLLISION
E_BUILD_PROVIDER_ALIAS_COLLISION
E_BUILD_PROVIDER_ALIAS_INVALID
E_BUILD_PROVIDER_ALTERNATIVE_UNSELECTED
E_BUILD_PROVIDER_AVAILABILITY_UNPINNED
E_BUILD_FEATURE_UNKNOWN
E_BUILD_FEATURE_CONFLICT
E_BUILD_FEATURE_REQUIREMENT_UNSATISFIED
E_BUILD_FEATURE_NON_MONOTONE
E_BUILD_DEP_UNRESOLVED
E_BUILD_DEP_RESOLVER_CONFLICT
E_BUILD_DEP_IDENTITY_UNPINNED
E_BUILD_PATH_DEP_CONTENT_UNPINNED
E_BUILD_TARGET_ARTIFACT_UNKNOWN
E_BUILD_TARGET_ARTIFACT_INCOMPATIBLE_USAGE
E_BUILD_WORKSPACE_PACKAGE_DUPLICATE
E_BUILD_WORKSPACE_TARGET_AMBIGUOUS
E_BUILD_LOCK_MISSING
E_BUILD_LOCK_MISMATCH
E_BUILD_LOCK_SCHEMA_INCOMPATIBLE
E_BUILD_LOCK_UPDATE_REQUIRED
E_BUILD_BACKEND_UNAVAILABLE
E_BUILD_ARTIFACT_UNSUPPORTED
E_BUILD_ARTIFACT_IDENTITY_MISMATCH
E_BUILD_EXPORT_SURFACE_UNSUPPORTED
E_BUILD_C_ABI_UNSUPPORTED_SURFACE
E_BUILD_INTERFACE_EQUIVALENCE_MISMATCH
E_BUILD_HOST_BINDING_UNAVAILABLE
E_BUILD_HOST_BINDING_UNPINNED
E_BUILD_HOST_BINDING_PRECISION_UNSUPPORTED
E_BUILD_ACTION_RESULT_STALE
E_BUILD_ACTION_ENV_UNPINNED
E_BUILD_NATIVE_ADAPTER_UNAVAILABLE
E_BUILD_NATIVE_LOAD_UNSUPPORTED
E_BUILD_RUNTIME_PREREQUISITE_UNRECORDED
E_BUILD_BRIDGE_UNAVAILABLE
E_BUILD_BRIDGE_CONTRACT_MISMATCH
E_BUILD_BRIDGE_PHASE_CYCLE
E_BUILD_BRIDGE_CALLBACK_UNSUPPORTED
E_BUILD_CODEGEN_INPUT_UNPINNED
E_BUILD_CODEGEN_OUTPUT_MISSING
E_BUILD_CODEGEN_OUTPUT_MISMATCH
E_BUILD_CODEGEN_UNDECLARED_INPUT
E_BUILD_CODEGEN_UNDECLARED_OUTPUT
E_BUILD_MACRO_DEP_UNPINNED
E_BUILD_MACRO_TRANSCRIPT_MISSING
E_BUILD_MACRO_TRANSCRIPT_MISMATCH
E_BUILD_TEST_EXPECTATION_MISMATCH
E_BUILD_TEST_UNSUPPORTED_BACKEND
E_BUILD_BENCHMARK_DATASET_UNPINNED
E_BUILD_BENCHMARK_ENVIRONMENT_UNPINNED
E_BUILD_ENTRYPOINT_MISSING
E_BUILD_ENTRYPOINT_INVALID
E_BUILD_DEPLOYMENT_UNREPRODUCIBLE
E_BUILD_PUBLISH_UNREPRODUCIBLE
E_BUILD_PUBLISH_PATH_DEPENDENCY_REJECTED
E_BUILD_QUERY_PLAN_NOT_FINAL
```

A diagnostic for interface equivalence mismatch MUST identify the equivalence group, compared targets, equivalence
mode, first differing fingerprint component, and relevant target/module/declaration provenance.

A diagnostic for feature conflict or unsatisfied requirement MUST identify the feature fixed point, the feature edge or
selection that introduced each relevant feature, and the target/package context.

A diagnostic for matrix expansion failure MUST identify the matrix target, selected axis values, include/exclude rule,
generated target name if one was computed, and generated-field provenance.

A diagnostic for codegen hermeticity failure MUST identify the codegen target, generator identity, undeclared input or
output, execution policy, and provenance of the generator selection.

A diagnostic for bridge phase cycle MUST identify the bridge target, phase outputs in the cycle, provider target or
artifact, consumer target or artifact, and the bridge realization.

A diagnostic for macro transcript mismatch MUST identify the macro package identity, macro symbol identity, invocation
origin, expected transcript identity, actual observation when available, and whether a lockfile or transcript update
mode can repair the mismatch.

A diagnostic for publish rejection MUST identify the publish target, rejected artifact, rejected dependency or
prerequisite when applicable, reproducibility vector, and publication policy that caused rejection.

A diagnostic for provider collision MUST identify every provider and the mechanism by which it was introduced.

A diagnostic for native binding failure MUST distinguish:

* binding-source failure;
* ABI-description failure;
* trusted-summary failure;
* adapter-mode failure;
* link failure;
* runtime-load failure;
* deployment failure.

A diagnostic for lockfile mismatch MUST identify:

* authored manifest source;
* manifest value provenance;
* previous lockfile entry;
* newly resolved value when available;
* command or mode needed to update the lockfile.

When exact config provenance is available, a build diagnostic SHOULD point to the smallest manifest expression whose edit
would repair the failing semantic value.

---

<!-- part.tooling_ide_support -->
# Part X. Tooling, IDE, and Interactive Semantic Services

<!-- tooling_ide -->
## 37. Tooling, IDE, and Interactive Semantic Services

<!-- tooling_ide.design -->
### 37.1 Design principles

Kappa tooling is an elaborator interface, not merely a text-editor integration.

A conforming IDE-oriented implementation MUST expose Kappa semantic information through dependency-tracked compiler queries, structured diagnostics, typed semantic identities, and source-preserving edits.

An IDE-oriented implementation MUST NOT maintain a second independently authoritative frontend for editor support.

In particular, editor-visible results for:

* name resolution;
* type inference;
* expected-type inference;
* quantity checking;
* borrow checking;
* region and capture checking;
* effect-row checking;
* trait and implicit search;
* normalization;
* coverage;
* termination;
* hole goals;
* diagnostics;
* refactoring legality; and
* source edit generation

MUST be computed from the same semantic model as batch compilation, or from an observationally equivalent slice of that model.

Presentation-level services MAY use lightweight mechanisms when they do not claim semantic authority. Such services include:

* syntax highlighting;
* bracket matching;
* indentation guides;
* outline grouping;
* snippets;
* simple lexical completions;
* theme scopes; and
* editor-local keybindings.

A presentation-level service MUST NOT cause source accepted by the editor to differ from source accepted by `kappa check`, `kappa analyze`, `kappa emit-interface`, or `kappa compile`.

<!-- tooling_ide.architecture -->
### 37.2 Shared language-service architecture

An implementation that provides IDE support SHOULD expose one shared Kappa language-service executable.

The reference executable name is:

```text
kappa language-server
````

Implementations MAY also expose aliases:

```text
kappa lsp
kappa ide
```

The language service SHOULD provide three public integration surfaces:

1. a standard Language Server Protocol surface;
2. a Kappa-specific semantic protocol surface; and
3. command-line semantic query commands for test harnesses, editor scripting, and reproducibility.

These surfaces are different transports over the same semantic query system.

The standard LSP surface is used for generic editor features.

The Kappa-specific semantic protocol is used for features that ordinary LSP cannot express precisely enough, including:

* goal display;
* hole refinement;
* case splitting;
* expected quantity and effect display;
* metavariable inspection;
* elaborator trace display;
* normalization with controlled unfolding;
* term and proof search;
* instance-search explanation;
* quantity-usage explanation;
* borrow-footprint explanation;
* erasure preview;
* macro expansion provenance; and
* structured source edits whose legality depends on Kappa semantics.

The command-line query surface is used to make the same behavior testable without a specific editor.

A language-service implementation MUST ensure that a result returned through one surface has the same semantic meaning as the corresponding result returned through another surface for the same source version, analysis session, build configuration, backend profile, dependency interface identities, and macro transcript.

<!-- tooling_ide.conformance_profiles -->
### 37.3 IDE conformance profiles

IDE support is divided into conformance profiles.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_3_1.tooling_core_profile -->
#### 37.3.1 Tooling Core profile

The Tooling Core profile applies to the compiler and language service.

An implementation satisfies the Tooling Core profile iff it provides:

* an analysis session model;
* source-versioned documents;
* dependency-tracked semantic queries;
* machine-readable structured diagnostics;
* source-preserving edits;
* standard LSP integration for ordinary language features;
* a Kappa-specific semantic protocol for dependent-type interactions;
* deterministic query results for fixed semantic inputs;
* cancellation and stale-result handling;
* recovery-aware answers for incomplete files; and
* golden-testable command-line equivalents for semantic queries.

The Tooling Core profile is the minimum acceptable profile for a serious Kappa implementation.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_3_2.first_class_client_profile -->
#### 37.3.2 First-Class Client profile

The First-Class Client profile applies to editor integrations maintained by the implementation.

A first-class Kappa client MUST provide:

* automatic language-server startup;
* project root discovery;
* build-profile selection;
* diagnostics;
* hover;
* go-to-definition;
* find references;
* semantic rename;
* completion;
* code actions;
* formatting integration;
* semantic tokens or equivalent highlighting;
* inlay hints where supported by the host editor;
* a Kappa InfoView or equivalent semantic side panel;
* commands for goal display, type display, expected type display, normalization, case split, hole refinement, and proof or term search;
* structured rendering of Kappa diagnostics and related origins; and
* safe application of Kappa-generated source edits.

A first-class client MUST NOT scrape human-readable compiler prose to recover semantic information.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_3_3.broad_compatibility_profile -->
#### 37.3.3 Broad Compatibility profile

The Broad Compatibility profile applies to editors that can consume the shared language service but do not expose enough extension surface for the complete Kappa interaction model.

A broad-compatibility client SHOULD provide:

* file-type registration;
* syntax highlighting;
* language-server launch;
* diagnostics;
* hover;
* completion;
* navigation;
* rename;
* code actions;
* formatting; and
* documentation for invoking any supported Kappa-specific commands.

If the editor cannot provide a Kappa InfoView, it SHOULD still expose goal, type, and diagnostic queries through commands, popups, scratch buffers, quick panels, or equivalent editor mechanisms.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_3_4.syntax_only_profile -->
#### 37.3.4 Syntax-Only profile

The Syntax-Only profile is not semantic IDE support.

A syntax-only integration MAY provide:

* file-type registration;
* TextMate grammar support;
* Tree-sitter grammar support;
* indentation rules;
* snippets; and
* basic formatting.

A syntax-only integration MUST NOT be described as Kappa IDE support unless the documentation states clearly that it is syntax-only.

<!-- tooling_ide.target_clients -->
### 37.4 Target editor and IDE matrix

The reference Kappa toolchain SHOULD target editor clients in the following priority order.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_4_1.required_first_class_clients -->
#### 37.4.1 Required first-class clients

The reference toolchain SHOULD maintain first-class clients for:

```text
Visual Studio Code
IntelliJ Platform
Neovim
Emacs
```

Visual Studio Code and IntelliJ Platform are primary mainstream IDE targets.

Neovim and Emacs are primary expert-editor targets and MUST NOT be treated as afterthoughts for Kappa, because Kappa's editing model depends heavily on goal-directed interaction, hole refinement, structural commands, and semantic side buffers.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_4_2.required_broad_compatibility_clients -->
#### 37.4.2 Required broad-compatibility clients

The reference toolchain SHOULD provide broad-compatibility integrations for:

```text
Helix
Zed
Theia-based IDEs
Eclipse IDE through LSP4E
Sublime Text through LSP integration
Kate or other KDE editors through LSP integration
classic Vim through an LSP-capable plugin
```

For these clients, the implementation SHOULD provide at least:

* language-server configuration;
* grammar configuration;
* file-type association;
* installation instructions;
* known feature limitations; and
* a list of Kappa-specific commands available through the host editor.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_4_3.conditional_clients -->
#### 37.4.3 Conditional clients

The reference toolchain MAY provide a Visual Studio extension when Kappa has a concrete Windows, .NET, enterprise, or Visual Studio-specific adoption requirement.

The reference toolchain SHOULD NOT prioritize a dedicated Visual Studio extension ahead of Neovim, Emacs, Helix, Zed, or Theia unless user demand or ecosystem integration justifies that cost.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_4_4.non_target_clients -->
#### 37.4.4 Non-target clients

JetBrains Fleet is not a named Kappa target.

An implementation MAY support any editor that can consume the standard Kappa language server, but the reference toolchain SHOULD NOT maintain a dedicated Fleet client.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_4_5.editor_neutral_support -->
#### 37.4.5 Editor-neutral support

The following artifacts SHOULD be editor-neutral:

```text
kappa language-server
tree-sitter-kappa
kappa.tmLanguage.json
kappa formatter
kappa semantic query CLI
kappa debug adapter, when implemented
```

Editor clients SHOULD be thin wrappers around these artifacts.

<!-- tooling_ide.standard_lsp_surface -->
### 37.5 Standard LSP surface

The Kappa language service SHOULD expose a standard Language Server Protocol server.

The LSP server SHOULD support at least the following conceptual features when the host client supports them:

* initialize / shutdown / exit;
* text document open, change, save, and close notifications;
* diagnostics;
* hover;
* completion;
* completion resolve;
* signature help;
* go-to-definition;
* go-to-declaration;
* go-to-type-definition;
* find references;
* document highlights;
* document symbols;
* workspace symbols;
* semantic tokens;
* inlay hints;
* code actions;
* code action resolve;
* rename;
* prepare rename;
* document formatting;
* range formatting;
* folding ranges;
* selection ranges;
* document links where useful;
* workspace commands; and
* progress and cancellation.

The LSP server MUST map standard LSP requests to Kappa semantic queries when the request is semantic.

Examples:

* LSP hover MUST use Kappa type, expected-type, quantity, effect, capture, and symbol queries.
* LSP rename MUST use declaration-symbol identity, not textual replacement.
* LSP diagnostics MUST be projections of Kappa structured diagnostics.
* LSP code actions MUST be projections of Kappa diagnostic fixes or semantic edit commands.
* LSP completion MUST use lexical, syntactic, and semantic completion sources according to the completion context.
* LSP semantic tokens MUST use semantic classification where available, not merely lexical classification.

If a standard LSP field cannot carry the full Kappa result, the LSP response MAY include a compact rendering and SHOULD provide a corresponding Kappa-specific command or data reference that allows the client to retrieve the complete structured result.

The LSP server MUST support request cancellation.

If an LSP request is answered using stale source, the response MUST either:

* be suppressed;
* be marked stale when the host protocol permits that marking; or
* be recomputed against the current source version before presentation.

The server MUST NOT present stale semantic answers as current.

<!-- tooling_ide.custom_protocol -->
### 37.6 Kappa-specific semantic protocol

The implementation SHOULD expose a Kappa-specific protocol over JSON-RPC or an observationally equivalent structured protocol.

The reference protocol name is:

```text
Kappa Interactive Semantic Protocol
```

The protocol version MUST be machine-readable.

A protocol response MUST NOT require clients to parse human-readable prose.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_6_1.common_request_envelope -->
#### 37.6.1 Common request envelope

A Kappa semantic request has the conceptual shape:

```text
KappaSemanticRequest =
    (schemaVersion       : Nat,
     protocolVersion     : ProtocolVersion,
     requestId           : RequestId,
     session             : AnalysisSessionId,
     document            : Option SourceIdentity,
     sourceVersion       : Option SourceVersion,
     position            : Option SourcePosition,
     range               : Option SourceRange,
     queryOptions        : QueryOptions,
     renderOptions       : RenderOptions,
     clientCapabilities  : ClientCapabilities)
```

`queryOptions` includes at least:

```text
allowRecovery              : Bool
allowStale                 : Bool
maxCandidates              : Option Nat
maxSearchDepth             : Option Nat
timeoutHintMillis          : Option Nat
normalizationMode          : Option NormalizationMode
unfoldingPolicy            : Option UnfoldingPolicy
includeGeneratedOrigins    : Bool
includeVerboseTrace        : Bool
```

`renderOptions` includes at least:

```text
showImplicitArguments      : Bool
showErasedArguments        : Bool
showUniverseLevels         : Bool
showQuantities             : Bool
showBorrowRegions          : Bool
showCaptureSets            : Bool
showEffectRows             : Bool
showInsertedTransports     : Bool
showGeneratedNames         : Bool
qualificationPolicy        : QualificationPolicy
aliasPolicy                : AliasPolicy
layoutPolicy               : LayoutPolicy
```

<!-- tooling_ide.x_6_2.common_response_envelope -->
#### 37.6.2 Common response envelope

A Kappa semantic response has the conceptual shape:

```text
KappaSemanticResponse =
    (schemaVersion      : Nat,
     protocolVersion    : ProtocolVersion,
     requestId          : RequestId,
     session            : AnalysisSessionId,
     sourceVersion      : Option SourceVersion,
     status             : QueryStatus,
     payload            : Option QueryPayload,
     diagnostics        : List Diagnostic,
     related            : List RelatedOrigin,
     recovery           : Option RecoverySummary,
     edits              : List SourceEditSet,
     stale              : Bool,
     dependencyDigest   : Option QueryDependencyDigest,
     trace              : Option TraceSummary)
```

`QueryStatus` is one of:

```text
ok
partial
stale
cancelled
unsupported
invalid-request
source-version-mismatch
analysis-blocked
internal-error
```

If `status` is `partial`, the response MUST explain which requested facts are unavailable.

If `status` is `analysis-blocked`, the response MUST include the diagnostic or blocked obligation that prevents a complete answer when such information is available.

If `status` is `unsupported`, the response MUST use a structured unsupported diagnostic.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_6_3.protocol_framing -->
#### 37.6.3 Protocol framing

All semantic protocol traffic MUST be framed.

A protocol endpoint MUST NOT emit unstructured human-readable text on the semantic response channel.

Logs, tracing text, progress messages, and human-readable diagnostics MUST use separate structured messages or separate output streams.

EOF, interruption, parse failure, unsupported commands, and shutdown MUST be reported through structured protocol replies when the protocol session is still alive.

A protocol error MUST NOT corrupt the response stream.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_6_4.required_custom_methods -->
#### 37.6.4 Required custom methods

The Kappa-specific protocol SHOULD expose at least the following methods:

```text
kappa/sessionInfo
kappa/buildProfile
kappa/sourceVersion
kappa/symbolAtPosition
kappa/semanticObjectAtPosition
kappa/typeAtRange
kappa/expectedAtPosition
kappa/contextAtPosition
kappa/goalAtPosition
kappa/holes
kappa/metavariables
kappa/normalize
kappa/elaboratedTerm
kappa/refineHole
kappa/caseSplit
kappa/missingClauses
kappa/fillTrivial
kappa/searchTerms
kappa/searchProofs
kappa/instanceSearch
kappa/implicitSearch
kappa/quantityUsage
kappa/borrowFootprint
kappa/captureSet
kappa/effectRow
kappa/coverage
kappa/termination
kappa/erasurePreview
kappa/macroExpansionTrace
kappa/featureGates
kappa/interfaceView
kappa/findUsages
kappa/prepareRename
kappa/rename
kappa/refactor
kappa/explainDiagnostic
kappa/applyDiagnosticFix
kappa/applySemanticEdit
```

Implementations MAY add methods.

Implementation-defined methods MUST use an implementation-reserved method prefix and MUST NOT collide with standardized `kappa/` methods.

<!-- tooling_ide.source_versions -->
### 37.7 Source identity, source versions, and stale results

Every semantic response over editor-opened source MUST be tied to a source identity and source version.

A source identity MUST distinguish files with equal display paths under different source roots.

A source version MUST distinguish at least:

* the source content;
* the file identity;
* the active build profile;
* the effective import environment at the queried position when relevant;
* the selected backend profile when relevant;
* the macro transcript when relevant; and
* dependency interface identities used by the query.

A source edit generated by the language service MUST include preconditions sufficient to detect stale application.

If a client requests a source edit against source version `V`, and the current editor buffer is no longer equivalent to `V`, the edit MUST NOT be silently applied.

The client may request remapping.

If remapping cannot be proven safe, the server or client MUST reject the edit with a structured stale-source diagnostic.

<!-- tooling_ide.positions_offsets -->
### 37.8 Positions, ranges, and offset conventions

The protocol MUST identify source positions and ranges using the same origin model as diagnostics.

A source range SHOULD include:

* source identity;
* start line;
* start column;
* end line;
* end column;
* optional byte offsets;
* optional UTF-8 offsets;
* optional UTF-16 code-unit offsets; and
* optional content digest for the covered range.

Line and column numbering in Kappa protocol JSON SHOULD match the diagnostic JSON convention.

If an editor transport uses a different convention, the client adapter MUST translate explicitly.

The protocol MUST NOT use an unlabeled integer offset whose unit is ambiguous.

<!-- tooling_ide.infoview -->
### 37.9 Kappa InfoView

A first-class Kappa editor client MUST provide a Kappa InfoView or equivalent interaction surface.

The InfoView is a structured semantic display associated with the active source position, selection, diagnostic, or hole.

The InfoView SHOULD provide at least the following panes or sections:

```text
Goal
Local Context
Implicit Context
Quantities
Borrow and Capture State
Effects
Metavariables
Diagnostics
Elaborated Term
Normalization
Instance Search
Proof and Term Search
Coverage
Termination
Erasure Preview
Macro and Generated-Origin Trace
Build Profile and Feature Gates
```

A client MAY merge panes, hide empty panes, or provide them as separate buffers, tool windows, webviews, quick panels, floating windows, or ordinary editor buffers.

The InfoView MUST consume structured Kappa protocol payloads.

The InfoView MUST NOT recover semantic structure by scraping rendered compiler prose.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_9_1.goal_pane -->
#### 37.9.1 Goal pane

The Goal pane displays the expected type and checking context at a hole or source position.

A goal display MUST include, when available:

* hole identity;
* source occurrence;
* expected type;
* expected quantity;
* expected effect row;
* expected capture set;
* expected modality obligations when an enabled modal extension applies;
* local explicit context;
* local implicit context;
* in-scope trait evidence;
* in-scope equality and boolean-refinement evidence;
* outstanding constraints;
* blocked metavariables;
* recovery status; and
* candidate refinements when requested.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_9_2.context_pane -->
#### 37.9.2 Context pane

A context entry has the conceptual shape:

```text
ContextEntry =
    (name              : RenderedName,
     symbol            : Option DeclarationSymbolId,
     kind              : ContextEntryKind,
     type              : RenderedSemanticFragment,
     quantity          : Option RenderedQuantity,
     access            : AccessMode,
     region            : Option RegionId,
     captureSet        : Option CaptureSet,
     effectRow         : Option RenderedEffectRow,
     implicitEligible  : Bool,
     runtimeErased     : Bool,
     origin            : Origin)
```

`ContextEntryKind` includes at least:

```text
explicit-local
implicit-local
trait-evidence
supertrait-projection
record-implicit-field
constructor-field
module-member
generated-helper
recovered-placeholder
```

The renderer SHOULD use stable user-oriented names.

The renderer MUST NOT show unstable raw helper names as the only identifier for a generated or lowered object.

If an internal generated helper must be shown, the renderer MUST use a stable explanatory name and SHOULD include the user-written source construct that generated it.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_9_3.diagnostic_pane -->
#### 37.9.3 Diagnostic pane

The Diagnostic pane displays the selected structured diagnostic.

It SHOULD expose:

* primary message;
* diagnostic code;
* diagnostic family;
* severity;
* primary origin;
* labels;
* related origins;
* payload;
* suppressed diagnostics;
* available fixes;
* explanation key;
* recovery provenance; and
* macro or generated-origin provenance.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_9_4.metavariable_pane -->
#### 37.9.4 Metavariable pane

The Metavariable pane displays unsolved and solved metavariables relevant to the active declaration or source position.

A metavariable entry has the conceptual shape:

```text
MetavariableInfo =
    (id                 : MetavariableId,
     userFacingName     : RenderedName,
     kind               : MetavariableKind,
     expectedClassifier : RenderedSemanticFragment,
     origin             : Origin,
     currentAssignment  : Option RenderedSemanticFragment,
     constraints        : List ConstraintSummary,
     blockedBy          : List ObligationId,
     defaultingStatus   : DefaultingStatus,
     recoveryStatus     : Option RecoverySummary)
```

The renderer SHOULD prefer source-level descriptions over raw metavariable IDs.

Raw IDs MAY be shown in verbose mode.

<!-- tooling_ide.holes -->
### 37.10 Holes and hole-driven development

Typed holes are first-class editing units.

A hole occurrence MUST have a stable hole identity within an analysis session.

Whitespace edits, comments, unrelated declarations, and unrelated import reordering SHOULD NOT change hole identities.

When a source edit structurally changes the enclosing declaration, the implementation SHOULD preserve hole identities when it can prove correspondence between old and new source holes.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_10_1.hole_goal_query -->
#### 37.10.1 Hole goal query

The method:

```text
kappa/goalAtPosition
```

returns goal information for the hole or incomplete expression at a position.

The result has the conceptual shape:

```text
GoalInfo =
    (hole              : HoleId,
     occurrences       : List Origin,
     enclosingDecl     : Option DeclarationSymbolId,
     expected          : RenderedSemanticFragment,
     expectedOrigin    : Option Origin,
     expectedQuantity  : Option RenderedQuantity,
     expectedEffectRow : Option RenderedEffectRow,
     expectedCapture   : Option CaptureSet,
     context           : List ContextEntry,
     implicitContext   : List ContextEntry,
     constraints       : List ConstraintSummary,
     metavariables     : List MetavariableInfo,
     inhabitance       : Option InhabitanceSummary,
     candidates        : List GoalCandidate,
     recovery          : Option RecoverySummary)
```

If the source position is not a hole but has a well-defined expected type, the implementation MAY return a synthetic goal.

If no meaningful goal is available, the method MUST return a structured `analysis-blocked` or `invalid-request` response rather than an empty prose string.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_10_2.hole_refinement -->
#### 37.10.2 Hole refinement

The method:

```text
kappa/refineHole
```

requests replacement of a hole by a candidate term, constructor skeleton, function skeleton, proof skeleton, or search result.

A refinement response MUST return either:

* a source edit set;
* several alternative source edit sets; or
* a precise rejection diagnostic.

A refinement edit MUST preserve unrelated concrete syntax and trivia where possible.

A refinement edit MUST preserve source indentation rules.

A refinement edit MUST include stale-source preconditions.

A refinement edit SHOULD introduce smaller holes when the refinement is partial.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_10_3.constructor_refinement -->
#### 37.10.3 Constructor refinement

When refining a goal by a constructor, the implementation SHOULD generate:

* the constructor name using a valid spelling in the current import environment;
* explicit arguments that must be user-written;
* named fields when named construction is more stable or readable;
* implicit arguments only when the render options request them;
* erased arguments only when the render options request them;
* subholes for unsolved explicit fields or arguments;
* required equality transports when they are unavoidable and render options permit them; and
* diagnostics when the constructor cannot satisfy the goal.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_10_4.case_splitting -->
#### 37.10.4 Case splitting

The method:

```text
kappa/caseSplit
```

generates a deterministic case split for a selected binder, expression, or hole.

A case split response MUST include:

* the scrutinee;
* the scrutinee type;
* generated cases;
* branch refinements;
* impossible branches;
* new holes;
* source edit set;
* coverage justification; and
* diagnostics for any branch that cannot be generated.

Case splitting over GADT-style data families MUST preserve family-argument refinements.

Case splitting over GADT constructors MUST expose family-argument equalities in generated branch contexts.

Case splitting over variants and rows MUST preserve residual-row binders when required.

Case splitting MUST be deterministic for fixed semantic inputs.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_10_5.missing_clause_generation -->
#### 37.10.5 Missing-clause generation

The method:

```text
kappa/missingClauses
```

generates clauses required by coverage checking.

A missing-clause response MUST be driven by the same coverage information used by the checker.

Generated clauses SHOULD include holes in branch bodies.

The response MUST identify any clauses omitted because the uncovered space is too large, symbolic, implementation-limited, or blocked by incomplete source.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_10_6.fill_trivial -->
#### 37.10.6 Fill trivial

The method:

```text
kappa/fillTrivial
```

attempts to fill a hole when the goal has an evident inhabitant.

Evident inhabitants include:

* `refl` for definitional equality goals;
* a unique constructor when the expected type is contractible;
* a unique local variable matching the expected type and quantity demand;
* a unique implicit evidence value;
* a unique erased proof under available assumptions;
* a unique record literal when all fields are solvable;
* a unique variant injection when the expected row is singleton; and
* implementation-defined solver-generated proofs whose provenance is structured.

If no unique inhabitant exists, the method MUST return candidates or a diagnostic explaining ambiguity or failure.

<!-- tooling_ide.type_expected_normalization -->
### 37.11 Type, expected-type, and normalization queries

<!-- tooling_ide.x_11_1.type_at_range -->
#### 37.11.1 Type at range

The method:

```text
kappa/typeAtRange
```

returns the elaborated type of the selected expression, pattern, declaration, type expression, or semantic object.

The response SHOULD include:

* compact rendering;
* fully explicit rendering or a stable reference to one;
* quantity of the selected value when relevant;
* access mode when relevant;
* capture set when relevant;
* effect row when relevant;
* inferred implicit arguments when relevant;
* elaborated core reference when available;
* source origin; and
* recovery status.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_11_2.expected_information_at_position -->
#### 37.11.2 Expected information at position

The method:

```text
kappa/expectedAtPosition
```

returns expected checking information at a source position.

The response SHOULD include:

* expected type;
* expected quantity;
* expected effect row;
* expected capture set;
* expected modality predicates;
* expected binder name when checking an argument;
* expected constructor field when checking a named field;
* expected record field when checking a record literal or update;
* expected trait goal when checking an implicit argument; and
* the origin of the expectation.

If no expected type is well-defined, the method MUST say so structurally.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_11_3.normalization -->
#### 37.11.3 Normalization

The method:

```text
kappa/normalize
```

normalizes a selected term, type, goal, or semantic fragment.

`NormalizationMode` includes at least:

```text
whnf
weak-head
head
simplify
normal-form
definitional-normal-form
```

`UnfoldingPolicy` includes at least:

```text
respect-opacity
unfold-transparent
unfold-selected
unfold-none
clarify-enabled
debug-unhide-enabled
```

A normalization response SHOULD include:

* input rendering;
* output rendering;
* mode;
* unfolding policy;
* names unfolded;
* names blocked by opacity;
* associated-member projections blocked by missing evidence;
* metavariables blocking normalization;
* effects of active `clarify` or `unhide`;
* diagnostics for illegal or unsupported requests; and
* a trace reference when verbose tracing is requested.

Normalization for IDE display MUST NOT change source acceptance.

<!-- tooling_ide.elaboration -->
### 37.12 Elaboration transparency

The method:

```text
kappa/elaboratedTerm
```

shows the elaborated form of a selected source expression, declaration, pattern, type, or hole.

An elaborated-term response SHOULD include:

* source rendering;
* elaborated rendering;
* inserted implicit arguments;
* inserted erased arguments;
* inserted quantity arguments;
* inserted equality transports;
* inserted default constructor fields;
* inserted record implicit fields;
* generated matcher or projection helpers;
* generated macro syntax;
* desugared forms;
* KCore reference when available; and
* provenance from elaborated constructs back to source origins.

The renderer SHOULD support toggles for:

* implicits;
* erased arguments;
* universe levels;
* quantities;
* captures;
* effect rows;
* generated names;
* macro expansions;
* desugarings; and
* fully explicit binders.

An implementation MUST NOT display a raw generated helper name whose spelling changes under unrelated edits unless verbose internal mode is enabled.

<!-- tooling_ide.completion -->
### 37.13 Completion

Completion is semantic when it claims semantic authority.

The language service SHOULD provide completion for at least:

* local variables;
* top-level declarations;
* module members;
* static type members;
* constructors;
* fields;
* constructor fields under refinement;
* trait members;
* associated static members;
* effect labels;
* effect operations;
* implicit arguments;
* named function arguments;
* named constructor arguments;
* record literal fields;
* record update fields;
* projection-section updates;
* imports;
* module paths;
* feature gates;
* quantities;
* region variables;
* holes and hole names;
* pattern heads;
* case clauses;
* missing branches;
* snippets for common syntactic forms; and
* proof or term candidates for holes.

A completion item has the conceptual shape:

```text
CompletionCandidate =
    (label              : String,
     insertText         : SourceText,
     replacementRange   : SourceRange,
     kind               : CompletionKind,
     semanticObject     : Option SemanticObjectId,
     symbol             : Option DeclarationSymbolId,
     type               : Option RenderedSemanticFragment,
     expectedMatch      : ExpectedMatch,
     quantityFit        : Option QuantityFit,
     effectFit          : Option EffectFit,
     importEdit         : Option SourceEditSet,
     additionalEdits    : List SourceEditSet,
     documentation      : Option Documentation,
     detail             : Option String,
     sortKey            : CompletionSortKey,
     applicability      : CompletionApplicability,
     recovery           : Option RecoverySummary)
```

`ExpectedMatch` includes at least:

```text
exact
subsumes
requires-implicit
requires-transport
requires-coercion
wrong-type
unknown
```

Completion ranking SHOULD prefer:

1. exact expected-type matches;
2. candidates satisfying expected quantity, borrow, capture, and effect requirements;
3. locally bound names;
4. names imported unqualified;
5. names available by qualification;
6. candidates requiring import insertion;
7. candidates requiring equality transport;
8. candidates requiring unsafe/debug features;
9. text-only snippets.

Completion ordering MUST be deterministic for fixed semantic inputs.

Typed completion MUST NOT hide all candidates merely because source is incomplete. It SHOULD degrade gracefully and mark candidates whose semantic fit is unknown.

<!-- tooling_ide.navigation -->
### 37.14 Navigation and semantic identity

Navigation MUST be keyed by semantic identity after resolution.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_14_1.go_to_definition -->
#### 37.14.1 Go-to-definition

A go-to-definition response SHOULD distinguish:

* source declaration site;
* signature site;
* implementation site;
* interface artifact site;
* generated declaration site;
* macro definition site;
* macro invocation site;
* constructor declaration site;
* field declaration site;
* trait member declaration site;
* instance declaration site;
* selected instance evidence site;
* static member declaration site;
* effect operation declaration site;
* unfolded transparent definition; and
* opaque blocked definition.

A client MAY present several navigation targets when several notions are meaningful.

For example, a selected overloaded trait member may provide navigation to:

* the overloaded member declaration;
* the trait declaration;
* the selected evidence value;
* the selected instance declaration; and
* the concrete method implementation.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_14_2.find_usages -->
#### 37.14.2 Find usages

Find-usages MUST be keyed by declaration-symbol identity, semantic object identity, or another typed identity.

Find-usages SHOULD classify usages as:

```text
direct-reference
qualified-reference
dotted-member-reference
constructor-use
pattern-use
field-projection
field-update
static-member-use
implicit-use
instance-selected-use
supertrait-projection-use
associated-member-use
macro-generated-use
unfolding-use
type-position-use
erased-use
runtime-use
import-use
export-use
```

A find-usages query SHOULD allow filtering by usage class.

Macro-generated usages MUST preserve provenance to the user-written source construct that generated them.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_14_3.rename -->
#### 37.14.3 Rename

Rename MUST be semantic.

A rename operation MUST:

* identify the declaration symbol being renamed;
* reject ambiguous selections;
* preserve alpha-equivalence;
* avoid capture;
* update all source occurrences in the selected rename scope;
* update import and export items when needed;
* update qualified and unqualified occurrences when appropriate;
* preserve unrelated formatting and trivia;
* report generated occurrences that cannot be edited directly;
* preserve operator and backtick identifier syntax; and
* include stale-source preconditions.

A rename operation MUST NOT rename by textual search.

<!-- tooling_ide.hovers_inlays_tokens -->
### 37.15 Hover, inlay hints, and semantic tokens

<!-- tooling_ide.x_15_1.hover -->
#### 37.15.1 Hover

A hover response SHOULD include:

* declaration name;
* declaration kind;
* compact type;
* fully explicit type reference;
* expected type when useful;
* quantity;
* borrow access mode;
* region and capture information;
* effect row;
* universe level;
* implicit arguments;
* erased arguments;
* trait evidence source;
* associated-member owner;
* documentation;
* visibility and opacity status;
* feature-gate requirements;
* source origin;
* interface origin; and
* diagnostics directly attached to the hovered construct.

Hover rendering SHOULD hide advanced details by default but make them available through render options.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_15_2.inlay_hints -->
#### 37.15.2 Inlay hints

The language service SHOULD provide inlay hints for:

* implicit arguments;
* erased arguments;
* inferred type arguments;
* universe arguments;
* inferred quantities;
* borrowed access regions;
* capture sets;
* effect rows;
* inserted equality transports;
* defaulted constructor fields;
* omitted implicit record fields;
* selected trait evidence;
* selected associated-member owner evidence;
* inferred return types for local definitions;
* named parameters for calls; and
* holes or metavariables.

A client SHOULD allow users to enable, disable, and filter classes of inlay hints.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_15_3.semantic_tokens -->
#### 37.15.3 Semantic tokens

Semantic tokens SHOULD distinguish at least:

* term variables;
* erased variables;
* linear variables;
* borrowed variables;
* type constructors;
* data constructors;
* traits;
* trait members;
* associated static members;
* modules;
* module aliases;
* record fields;
* constructor fields;
* effect labels;
* effect operations;
* quantities;
* region variables;
* universe variables;
* holes;
* metavariables;
* macros;
* generated names;
* unresolved names;
* recovery placeholders; and
* inactive feature-gated syntax.

Semantic token classification MUST be consistent with Kappa name resolution when semantic data is available.

<!-- tooling_ide.diagnostics -->
### 37.16 Diagnostics and code actions

Editor diagnostics MUST be projections of Kappa structured diagnostics.

A tooling diagnostic query MUST return the same structured diagnostic records that batch compilation would produce for the same source inputs, build configuration, backend profile, macro transcript, dependency interface identities, and implementation version, modulo query scope and incomplete-file recovery.

A language service MUST NOT maintain a separate diagnostic engine whose results can disagree with batch compilation.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_16_1.lsp_diagnostic_projection -->
#### 37.16.1 LSP diagnostic projection

When projecting Kappa diagnostics into LSP diagnostics, the server SHOULD preserve:

* diagnostic code;
* diagnostic family;
* severity;
* primary range;
* related origins;
* tags;
* source;
* explanation key;
* fix availability; and
* data reference for retrieving the complete diagnostic.

If the LSP client cannot display all related origins or payload fields, the Kappa-specific protocol MUST make them retrievable.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_16_2.code_actions -->
#### 37.16.2 Code actions

A code action MUST be backed by one of:

* a structured diagnostic fix;
* a semantic refactoring query;
* a hole-refinement query;
* a case-split query;
* a missing-clause query;
* an import insertion query;
* a formatting query; or
* an implementation-defined structured edit query.

A code action MUST include:

* title;
* kind;
* applicability;
* source version;
* source edits;
* preconditions;
* expected postcondition when known;
* related diagnostic when any; and
* whether the edit is machine-applicable.

A client MUST NOT mark a code action as applied unless every edit in its edit set was applied atomically.

<!-- tooling_ide.kappa_specific_views -->
### 37.17 Kappa-specific semantic views

A first-class client SHOULD expose the following Kappa-specific semantic views.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_17_1.quantity_and_linearity_view -->
#### 37.17.1 Quantity and linearity view

The quantity view displays usage obligations for the active declaration, hole, or selected expression.

It SHOULD include:

* binder quantity;
* inferred demand;
* `QuantitySatisfies` obligations;
* positive lower-bound obligations;
* use sites;
* unused paths;
* duplicated use paths;
* discharged linear obligations;
* consumed paths;
* restored paths;
* droppable residues;
* unsolved symbolic quantity obligations; and
* suggested repairs.

For a linearity diagnostic, the client SHOULD allow navigation among all related use sites.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_17_2.borrow_region_and_capture_view -->
#### 37.17.2 Borrow, region, and capture view

The borrow view displays:

* borrow root;
* borrowed path;
* static footprint;
* dependency-expanded footprint;
* region identity;
* region-introducing construct;
* capture set;
* live borrow range;
* overlapping operations;
* borrow escape sites;
* consumed paths;
* restored paths; and
* values whose hidden environment mentions a region.

A borrow view MUST distinguish a quantity from borrowed access.

The symbol `&` MUST NOT be rendered as a quantity.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_17_3.effect_view -->
#### 37.17.3 Effect view

The effect view displays:

* current effect row;
* expected effect row;
* handled effects;
* residual effects;
* effect labels;
* effect interfaces;
* operations available at a label;
* operation invocations;
* handler cases;
* resumptions;
* backend capability requirements; and
* effect-row obligations such as `ContainsEff`, `LacksEff`, and `SplitEff`.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_17_4.instance_and_implicit_search_view -->
#### 37.17.4 Instance and implicit-search view

The instance-search view displays:

* implicit goal;
* local implicit candidates;
* projected supertrait candidates;
* intrinsic solver result;
* global instance candidates;
* candidates rejected by head mismatch;
* candidates rejected by failed premises;
* candidates rejected by visibility or opacity;
* candidates rejected by coherence;
* recursive search depth;
* cycles;
* selected evidence;
* associated static members used during normalization; and
* ambiguity or incoherence explanations.

The view MUST distinguish local implicit resolution from global instance search.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_17_5.associated_member_normalization_view -->
#### 37.17.5 Associated-member normalization view

When normalization is blocked by associated-member owner evidence, the view SHOULD display:

* associated member;
* owner expression;
* governing trait;
* required trait goal;
* evidence candidates;
* selected or missing evidence;
* fallback unreduced type;
* source expression requiring normalization; and
* related diagnostics.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_17_6.termination_view -->
#### 37.17.6 Termination view

The termination view displays:

* recursive SCC;
* recursive definitions;
* recursive call sites;
* selected strategy;
* structural parameters;
* user-written `decreases` clause;
* inferred measure candidates;
* caller and callee measures;
* size-change matrices when available;
* failing cycles;
* accepted decreases proofs;
* implementation limits; and
* suggested `decreases` clauses.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_17_7.coverage_view -->
#### 37.17.7 Coverage view

The coverage view displays:

* scrutinee type;
* constructors or variants covered;
* missing constructors or variants;
* family-argument refinements;
* impossible branches;
* reachable residual shapes;
* guard effects on coverage;
* missing cases; and
* source edits for generated missing clauses.

<!-- tooling_ide.x_17_8.erasure_preview -->
#### 37.17.8 Erasure preview

The erasure preview displays the runtime-relevant shape of a selected declaration or expression after erasing:

* quantity-`0` arguments;
* compile-time-only values;
* erased proofs;
* erased indices;
* erased implicit fields;
* erased trait evidence when representation analysis permits it;
* meta-phase constructs; and
* generated helper artifacts that do not survive runtime lowering.

The preview MUST distinguish source-level erasure from backend optimization.
